case_name: Darren Schuhmacher, M.D. v. Glenn Broyles

summary.brief: Darren Schuhmacher, M.D. v. Glenn Broyles. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: On August 27, 2020, Broyles underwent a lumbar spinal epidural steroid injection from Eric Jenkins, M.D.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Broyles alleges that he immediately began having signs and symptoms indicating a dural tear and leak of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Jenkins performed a blood patch procedure on September 1, 2020, but the procedure did not relieve Broyles’s pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Schuhmacher then performed another blood patch procedure on September 9, 2020.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Lutz stated the standard of care for epidural blood patch procedures: In performing either an epidural steroid injection or an epidural blood patch, the standard of care applicable to Drs. Jenkins and Schuhmacher required that they be familiar with spinal anatomy.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Dr. Lutz explained that during the blood patch procedure performed on September 1, 2020, Dr. Jenkins injected the blood patch directly into Mr. Broyles’ spinal canal—not into the epidural space, but subdural.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Mr. Broyles was eventually diagnosed with a condition known as 'cauda equina syndrome.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr. Broyles suffered permanent injury to these nerves and as a result has permanent pain and disability.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court’s February 16, 2023, order denying Dr. Schumacher’s objections and motion to dismiss is affirmed.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Signs and symptoms indicating a dural tear and leak of cerebrospinal fluid
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient underwent a lumbar spinal epidural steroid injection and immediately began having signs and symptoms indicating a dural tear and leak of cerebrospinal fluid.

focused_exam.neurological: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Suspected dural tear with cerebrospinal fluid leak

requestables.name: MRI of lumbar spine
requestables.description: MRI to assess for blood in spinal canal

reveal.decision_impact: Confirms nerve compression and potential injury

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with symptoms of dural tear and CSF leak

timeline_phases.name: Blood Patch Procedures
timeline_phases.description: Blood patch procedures performed to address CSF leak

key_events.event: First blood patch procedure by Dr. Jenkins
Second blood patch procedure by Dr. Schuhmacher

decision_points.prompt: What is the correct technique for performing a blood patch?

options.description: Inject blood into the epidural space
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires injection into epidural space

options.description: Inject blood into the spinal canal
options.clinical_reasoning: Incorrect technique leading to nerve injury

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant injected blood into spinal canal

expected_action_court.description: Blood should have been injected into the epidural space

explanation.what_should_have_happened: Blood should have been injected into the epidural space

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed cauda equina syndrome with permanent nerve injury

key_findings: Breach of duty in injecting blood into spinal canal
Causation established - nerve injury due to improper technique

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Proper technique would have avoided nerve injury

factual_timeline.event: Patient underwent lumbar spinal epidural steroid injection

treatments.outcome: CSF leak stopped but nerve injury occurred
